title: ProcScript code formatting
html:
ProcScript code formatting

Add to My Topics
Add selected topic and subtopicsAdd Topic & Subtopics with topic headAdd entire publication

Download PDF

  • Last UpdatedMärz 20, 2026
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

With ProcScript code formatting options, you can correct or improve your code indentation to increase code readability and understanding. You can apply code formatting on a selected portion of a code using the Format Selection option, or else you can format the entire document using the Format Document option.

Format Selection

To format a selected portion of code, you can use either the mouse or keyboard. You need to right-click to bring up the popup menu and click Format Selection to format the current selection. You can always undo formatting actions with Ctrl+Z.

Additionally, you can also use the default shortcut key defined for Format Selection feature in the [userkeys] section of the usys.ini file. The default shortcut key defined in the usys.ini file is Alt+F as shown in the following block:

[USERKEYS] 
code.formatselection=Alt+F

Format Document

To format the entire document from the first line to the last line, you can select the Format Document menu option. You can undo the action using Ctrl+Z if the resulting format does not meet expectations. The indentation rules applied by this option are consistent with those used by the Format Selection menu option.

Additionally, you can also use the default shortcut key defined for Format Document feature in the [userkeys] section of the usys.ini file. The default shortcut key defined in the usys.ini file is Alt+Shift+F as shown in the following block:

[USERKEYS] 
code.formatdocument=Alt+Shift+F

Formatting rules

Indentation rules

What can be observed is that we have zero-based keywords whereby zero means the starting point. The following keywords are marked as zero-based, and thus indentation does not apply to these keywords:

  • Trigger
  • Operation
  • Params
  • EndParams
  • Variables
  • EndVariables
  • WebOperation
  • Throws
  • returns
  • end
  • defines
  • Other pre-compiler definitions, such as #comment, #ifdef, etc.
Single indentation parts
  • Code in general
  • Variables declaration block
  • Parameter declaration block

Indentation increase

The following keywords increase the indentation by the number of spaces defined in the settings.

  • If - The next lines will be indented with the indentation definition.
  • Else - Uses indentation level of if for the coming lines. Else comes on the same level as the connected if statement.
  • Elseif - Uses indentation level of 'if'.
  • While
  • Repeat
  • Selectcase
  • Elsecase
  • Case - Any subsequent case does not increase the indentation.
  • For

Indentation decrease

The following keywords decrease the indentation by the number of spaces defined in the settings.

  • Endif
  • Endwhile
  • Endselectcase
  • Until
  • Endfor

Key features

The source code editor provides the following formatting features on a selection:

  1. It indents all possible code blocks, such as If endif, while, endwhile, params, variables, etc.
  2. It does not indent block data, line continuation in strings, and JavaScript inside proc-script.
  3. The Undo action restores the code to the original state of the code before the formatting action.
  4. Code indentation is not currently applicable to the HTML editor.

Behavior and limitations

The formatting feature relies on ProcScript keywords, such as If, endif, while, and endwhile to determine indentation. However, limitations arise when the selection lacks any keyword. If the selection comprises only a comment line or a single if line, the formatter might produce unexpected results. To prevent this, ensure the selection provides ample context. For example, select a complete operation or begin the selection on a formatting keyword. Initiating the selection with an if serves as an anchor point, ensuring the rest of the selection is formatted relative to this keyword. This approach to selection helps avoid unexpected outcomes. In cases where the result is not as expected, you can easily undo the change with CTRL+Z or re-select a larger portion and apply formatting again. Expanding the selection provides the formatter with more context, resulting in a more accurate outcome.

Pre-compiler directives

When we have #define or #comment pre-compiler directives as part of the regular code, the directives will follow the indentation of the code.

For example:

#startdefine 1
operation $aap
  #define DEF1 = value1 ;is indented
  #ifdefined CLASSIC
    #comment Some comment
    if (something = <DEF1>)
      putmess $wim
    endif
   #endif
#enddefine
end

If the #define, #include, and #comment lines are located outside of the operation in the code, the formatting will place these lines at position zero.

Comment lines

When only a comment line is selected in an operation, trigger or entry, the comment line moves to position zero. The editor does not know where you are at this moment and assumes you are selecting outside the operation. As soon there is a formatting keyword in the selection, such as if or while the editor assumes you are in an operation. These proc-script keywords trigger the code formatting to let the comment lines follow the standard code indentation rules.

Block data and line continuation

Lines in the block data are not formatted when Format Selection is applied, the same is true for code which is part of a line continuation.

Line continuation example:

Operation Hello
  While ($noot)
    if ($dogs)
      $message_string = "The quick brown fox%\
jumps over the lazy dogs"
    endif
  endwhile
end

The line after the line continuation is not touched.

Block data example:

noot:blockdata+
This is 
  some 
  block
    data
+

When this is part of the selection, the formatter skips the text between the '+' signs.

Behavior and limitations The formatting feature relies on ProcScript keywords, such as If , endif , while , and endwhile to determine indentation. Pre-compiler directives When we have #define or #comment pre-compiler directives as part of the regular code, the directives will follow the indentation of the code. Comment lines When only a comment line is selected in an operation, trigger or entry, the comment line moves to position zero. Block data and line continuation Lines in the block data are not formatted when Format Selection is applied, the same is true for code which is part of a line continuation. Line continuation example: Explain this code Operation Hello While ($noot) if ($dogs) $message_string = "The quick brown fox%\ jumps over the lazy dogs" endif endwhile end Copy The line after the line continuation is not touched. Block data example: Explain this code noot:blockdata+ This is some block data + Copy When this is part of the selection, the formatter skips the text between the ' + ' signs. Table 1.
